nodes.description: Vessel fragility and small-vessel dysfunction produce both non-hemorrhagic injury (white matter hyperintensities, microinfarcts, enlarged perivascular spaces) and hemorrhagic lesions (strictly lobar cerebral microbleeds, convexity subarachnoid hemorrhage, cortical superficial siderosis, and lobar intracerebral hemorrhage), and contributes to cognitive decline. This is the final stage of the progression framework.

evidence.reference_title: Progression of cerebral amyloid angiopathy: a pathophysiological framework.
evidence.snippet:
(stage four) 
appearance of haemorrhagic brain lesions

evidence.reference_title: Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy: Clinical Presentation, Sequelae and Neuroimaging Features-An Update.
evidence.snippet:
neuroradiologic findings of CAA include cortical and subcortical microbleeds 
(MB), cortical subarachnoid hemorrhage (cSAH) and cortical superficial siderosis

nodes.description: Exogenous Abeta assemblies introduced via contaminated cadaveric tissue can seed vascular and parenchymal Abeta pathology after long incubation periods, consistent with prion-like templated misfolding, producing acquired (iatrogenic) CAA and Alzheimer-type pathology decades after exposure.

evidence.reference_title: Iatrogenic Alzheimer's disease in recipients of cadaveric pituitary-derived growth hormone.
evidence.snippet:
We 
previously reported human transmission of Aβ pathology and CAA in relatively 
young adults who had died of iatrogenic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (iCJD) after 
childhood treatment with cadaver-derived pituitary growth hormone (c-hGH) 
contaminated with both CJD prions and Aβ seeds.

evidence.reference_title: Iatrogenic Alzheimer's disease in recipients of cadaveric pituitary-derived growth hormone.
evidence.snippet:
As propagating Aβ 
assemblies may exhibit structural diversity akin to conventional prions

nodes.description: Reduced clearance of soluble Abeta along perivascular (intramural periarterial) drainage pathways leads to its progressive accumulation in cortical and leptomeningeal vessel walls. APOE modulates Abeta processing, aggregation, and clearance, making APOE genotype the dominant common genetic determinant of sporadic CAA.

evidence.reference_title: Deciphering the role of APOE in cerebral amyloid angiopathy: from genetic insights to therapeutic horizons.
evidence.snippet: it plays a known role in processing, production, aggregation, and clearance

nodes.description: Aβ deposition in the walls of small and medium cortical and leptomeningeal vessels of the cerebrum and cerebellum is the defining lesion of CAA. This is the first stage of a multi-decade progression framework.

cell_types: Vascular smooth muscle cell of the brain vasculature

evidence.reference_title: Deciphering the role of APOE in cerebral amyloid angiopathy: from genetic insights to therapeutic horizons.
evidence.snippet:
characterized by the deposition of amyloid-β 
(Aβ) peptides in the walls of medium and small vessels of the brain and 
leptomeninges

evidence.reference_title: Progression of cerebral amyloid angiopathy: a pathophysiological framework.

nodes.description: Progressive vascular amyloid deposition is accompanied by loss of vascular smooth muscle cells, vessel-wall thickening, and altered cerebrovascular physiology, weakening the vessel wall. Hypertension is a major non-genetic trigger that promotes vessel-wall weakening and hemorrhage. These changes progress through altered cerebrovascular physiology to non-hemorrhagic brain injury.

cell_types: Vascular smooth muscle cell of the brain vasculature

evidence.reference_title: Progression of cerebral amyloid angiopathy: a pathophysiological framework.
evidence.snippet:
(stage two) 
alteration of cerebrovascular physiology, (stage three) non-haemorrhagic brain injury
